coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of CuMnO2/graphene quantum dot nanocomposites as novel electrode materials for high performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Ashourdan, Mohammad
Semnani, Abolfazl
Hasanpour, Foroozan
Moosavifard, Seyyed Ebrahim
coredata.dc:description: Transition metal oxides are one of the promising materials for energy storage because they have rich redox reactions with good stability. In this study, CuMnO2 nanocrystals and CuMnO2/graphene quantum dot (GQD) composite were synthesized by hydrothermal method and used in constructing a new supercapacitor. GQD has been applied to increase specific capacity and improve efficiency of the supercapacitor. Various methods were used to identify the synthesized nanocomposite and to study its morphology, structure and surface area. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to record and track the electrochemical behavior of the synthesized nanocomposite used as an electrode material. The study of changes in the capacity of CuMnO2/GQD and CuMnO2 electrodes during 5000 consecutive charge/discharge cycles showed that the stability of the electrode made of nanocomposite is higher and its capacity after this number of cycles reached 83.3%, while the capacity of the electrode made with CuMnO2 nanoparticles has reached 65.4%. The specific capacity of CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite and CuMnO2 nanoparticles at a current density of 1 A g−1 was calculated to be 520.2 and 381.5 C g−1, respectively. The maximum specific energy of CuMnO2/GQD//AC asymmetric supercapacitor was obtained at a specific power of 1108.1 W kg−1 equal to 47.9 Wh Kg−1. Asymmetric supercapacitor capacitance decreased by only less than 13.3% after 5000 charge and discharge cycles, which is a very good cycle life compared to similar materials. All these results indicate that the CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite can be deliberated as a possible option for fast and stable supercapacitor.

Investigation of electrochemical behavior of synthesized nanocomposites

(a) CuMnO2/GQD//AC supercapacitor cycle life at current density 7 A g−1, (b) Nyquist curves of CuMnO2/GQD//AC supercapacitor before and after cycle stability, and (c) Ragone diagram related to the energy density versus the power density of the ASC device.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of CuMnO2/graphene quantum dot nanocomposites as novel electrode materials for high performance supercapacitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transition metal oxides are one of the promising materials for energy storage because they have rich redox reactions with good stability. In this study, CuMnO2 nanocrystals and CuMnO2/graphene quantum dot (GQD) composite were synthesized by hydrothermal method and used in constructing a new supercapacitor. GQD has been applied to increase specific capacity and improve efficiency of the supercapacitor. Various methods were used to identify the synthesized nanocomposite and to study its morphology, structure and surface area. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to record and track the electrochemical behavior of the synthesized nanocomposite used as an electrode material. The study of changes in the capacity of CuMnO2/GQD and CuMnO2 electrodes during 5000 consecutive charge/discharge cycles showed that the stability of the electrode made of nanocomposite is higher and its capacity after this number of cycles reached 83.3%, while the capacity of the electrode made with CuMnO2 nanoparticles has reached 65.4%. The specific capacity of CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite and CuMnO2 nanoparticles at a current density of 1 A g−1 was calculated to be 520.2 and 381.5 C g−1, respectively. The maximum specific energy of CuMnO2/GQD//AC asymmetric supercapacitor was obtained at a specific power of 1108.1 W kg−1 equal to 47.9 Wh Kg−1. Asymmetric supercapacitor capacitance decreased by only less than 13.3% after 5000 charge and discharge cycles, which is a very good cycle life compared to similar materials. All these results indicate that the CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite can be deliberated as a possible option for fast and stable supercapacitor.

ce:section.ce:para: In many application fields, batteries and supercapacitors are two effective and practical technologies for converting and storing electrochemical energy [1]. Supercapacitors have been used as a new type of energy storage systems for mobile electronics, electric vehicles, portable power supplies and various power systems [2]. Moreover, Supercapacitors as powerful energy storage devices have received much consideration because of their attractive properties for example great safety, high power density, low maintenance cost, short charging time and long life, unlike conventional batteries [3–5]. However, the practical application of commercial supercapacitors that exist today is limited because their energy density is relatively low, and so huge efforts have been made to increase their energy density [2,6]. Supercapacitors can be divided into three groups containing electric double layer capacitors (EDLC), pseudocapacitors and hybrid supercapacitors. The basis of this division is the concept of energy storage [7,8]. Among them, electrostatic energy storage in EDLCs is the result of the adsorption and repelling of electrolyte ions at the electrode surface [9]. The non-Faradaic electrostatic adsorption process on the electrode surface is fast and reversible, resulting in high power density and limited energy density [10]. Various nano- materials have been commonly used as electrodes for supercapacitors application such as carbon-based materials for example carbon quantum dots [11], conducting polymers and metal oxides [12]. In EDLCs, carbon-based materials with an extraordinary specific surface area for instance carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [13], activated carbon (AC) [14], carbon aerogels [15], and graphene [16] are applied. Pseudocapacitors, also called Faradaic supercapacitors differ from EDLCs, their performance is due to the fast and reversible Faradaic reactions or the oxidation–reduction reaction between the electrolyte and the electrode material at or near surface of the electrode [8,9,17]. In faradic electrochemical processes, the charge passes through a double layer, which increases the energy density as well as the capacity [18,19]. Despite these advantages, relatively low power densities and their cycle instabilities are major disadvantages compared to EDLCs [20,21]. Oxides of metals for instance MnO2 and RuO2, and electrically conductive polymers for example polyanilines, polypyrroles and polythiophene are the most common materials used to make supercapacitor electrode materials [22–25]. The result of the simultaneous use of EDLC and Faradic pseudocapacitors is a hybrid supercapacitor that has excellent electrochemical performance due to having the advantages of both at the same time [26,27].

Although carbon materials such as graphene sheets are inexpensive and have a high surface area with good chemical, thermal stability and excellent electronic and mechanical features [28], but they have some functional problems such as low energy density and limited available levels for energy storage [29–31]. To overcome these problems, the use of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, has played an important role [32]. GQDs are well dispersed in many organic solvents [33]. In this structure ion transfer is easy. So, ions can easily access and penetrate into the structure, which is an advantage over other graphene materials [34]. New generation supercapacitor materials with flexible thin film have a better and more hopeful future with the development of GQDs that have interesting properties such as large surface area, excellent mechanical and conductivity properties [35,36].

Bimetallic oxides have a wider potential window for redox reactions than single metal oxides and can deliver better capacitive performance [37–39]. Metal oxides such as CuMO2 (M = Cr, Al, Ga, Fe, Mn, etc.), known as delafossite type metal oxides, have interesting electrical and optical properties [40–44]. CuMnO2 has been used in many fields such as the manufacture of photovoltaic devices, photo-evolution of H2 and the elimination of CO and NOx [44]. Lately, CuMnO2 nanoparticles have been reported as an electrode material. To prepare a symmetric supercapacitor non-conductive connections were used to reduce the effective surface area and conductivity of the electrode [45].

As best of our knowledge, there is no report on the synthesis and application of CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite as supercapacitor electrode material. Therefore, in this work for the first time we have designed and synthesized CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite as a low cost and eco-friendly efficient electrode material for the development of asymmetric supercapacitors. Simultaneous use of CuMnO2 and GQD has resulted in the production of an energy storage device with good electrochemical conductivity, high capacity, and great stability.

Cu(NO3)2 ·H2O, Mn(NO3)2 ·4H2O, citric acid, AC, nafion, and carbon black were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethanol, NaOH, KOH, HCl 37% were obtained from the Merck. Water was distilled twice in all experimental steps and there was no need to purify the chemicals before use.

GQDs were prepared according to previous report [46]. In summary, 2.0 g of citric acid was first heated to 200 °C for 30 min until an orange liquid appeared. Then 100.0 ml of sodium hydroxide solution 10.0 mg per liter was added slowly and dropwise to the orange liquid to adjust the pH to 9, Because in this pH, the best the yield and size was achieved according to the previous report [47] and the solution was stirred at the same time. Finally, the synthesized GQDs were kept in the refrigerator at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CuMnO2 and CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: CuMnO2 nanocrystals were synthesized by hydrothermal process according to the previously reported method with slight changes [48]. In short, Cu(NO3)2 ·3H2O and Mn(NO3)2 ·4H2O (15.0 mmol of each) were dissolved in 70 ml of distilled water. Then 4.40 g of NaOH was dissolved in 20 ml of distilled water and added to the above solution at room temperature and stirred for 15 min. The completely uniform solution was poured into an autoclave and heated 24 h at 80 °C. The resulting precipitate was reddish-brown and washed several times with water and ethanol to remove excess reactants from the precipitate. The precipitate was then placed in an oven at 55 °C to dry completely in 8 h

For the synthesis of CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite, all the above steps were performed, with the difference that 2.0 ml of GQD was added when transferring the solution to the autoclave. A schematic is shown of the synthesis steps of nanocomposites in Fig. 1 .

Various methods were used to study the morphology and structure of the nanoparticles synthesized in this work, such as transmission electron microscope (TEM) made by the German company Zeiss, model EM10C-100 Kv, High-Resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Japan company JEOL, model JEM-2100F) and field emission scanning electron microscopes (FE-SEM) connected with energy-dispersive X-Ray (EDX) analysis, model Philips XL3. The specific surface area and the size distribution of the pores was investigated by the ASAP-2010 micrometric device. Bruker D8 Advance XRD (X-ray diffractometer), Raman spectrometer made by the Japan company HORIBA model XploRA PLUS and the Bruker Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrometer were used to identify the synthesized material.

A three-electrode cell containing auxiliary electrode (Pt), reference electrode (saturated Ag/AgCl), and working electrode of nickel foam coated with synthesized materials were applied for all electrochemical measurements. A potentiostat/galvanostat Autolab PGSTAT302N, made by Metrohm Netherlands combined with FRA32M module was used for electrochemical measurements including cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS).

To prepare the electrode, the active substance suspension (synthesized nanocomposite, carbon black, and nafion solution (2% w/w in ethanol) 75, 20, and 5 parts by weight, respectively, were added to 1 ml of a blend of distilled ethanol and water (1:1 ratio) and placed in an ultrasonicator bath for 30 min to take a homogeneous suspension. To make the electrode, a piece of nickel foam with dimensions of 0.5 × 0.5 cm was cut and the suspension prepared in the previous step was sprayed on its surface to completely cover the surface. The electrodes were transferred to an oven at 55 °C to dry for 12 h. The mass of the electrode was measured before and after coating, and then the coated mass (including active substance, carbon black and nafion) was calculated. The coated masses for the electrode prepared with CuMnO2/GQD and CuMnO2 nanomaterials were equal to 0.9 (equivalent to 3.6 mg cm−2) and 0.8 mg (equivalent to 3.2 mg cm−2), respectively.

A three-electrode system was used for the tests, and the electrolyte was an aqueous solution of KOH 3.0 M. An Ag/AgCl saturated electrode and a platinum electrode were applied as reference and auxiliary electrodes, sequentially. They were placed in the electrolyte solution for 30 min before using. The tests were performed at ambient temperature and pressure.

To make an asymmetric supercapacitor, AC and CuMnO2/GQD nanoporous electrodes were used as the negative and positive electrodes, and polyethylene (PE) paper for alkaline batteries was used as a separator. To obtain the maximum potential window, the charge stored on both positive and negative electrodes is necessary to be equal (
q + = q −
). So, the charge balance theory is used to determine the mass ratio of positive and negative electrodes, which is a necessity, as below:
(1)
q = m × C ×
Δ
V
(2)
m +

C −
×
Δ
V −

V +
Where q is stored charge (C), C is the capacity (F g−1), m is the mass of electrode material (g), ΔE is the potential range (V).

Therefore, mass ratio m+/m−= 0.34 was calculated. Here, according to the obtained ratio, the amount of active substance was 4.3 on the negative electrode and 1.4 mg cm−2 on the positive electrode. Therefore, the total mass for use on the positive and negative electrodes in the calculations was 5.7 mg cm−2.

The FE-SEM was applied for study the nanomaterial morphology. The FE-SEM images shown in Fig. 2 (a, b) are related to CuMnO2 crystals which exhibited a typical hexagonal morphology of delafossite oxides which includes nanoplate-like geometric structures.

Fig. 2(c) and (d) display the FE-SEM images of CuMnO2/GQD. As shown, the particle size of CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposites is smaller than that of CuMnO2 nanocrystals. Since graphene quantum dot was used only as the nucleus to form the CuMnO2 spinel, we do not expect to see graphene quantum dot. The TEM images for CuMnO2/GQD are shown in Fig. 3 (a) and (b) confirming the dimensions and morphology of the FESEM images. To get more insight into the morphology of CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite, its morphology was investigated by HRTEM as shown in Fig. 3c. It can be found that the GQDs act as cores for the growth of CuMnO2. In the inset of Fig. 3c, the white halo around the graphene quantum dot shows the presence of CuMnO2.

EDX mapping analysis was performed to elemental study of CuMnO2. The EDX spectrum presented in Fig. 4 a Proves the existence of O, Mn and Cu elements in CuMnO2 nanocrystals. The inserted table in this figure is weight percentage and atomic percentage analysis of the elements. Also, elemental mapping of Cu, Mn, and O were shown in Fig. 4a (inset). It is clear that the difference in colors shows the presence of different elements.

The EDX spectrum presented in Fig. 4b confirms the existence of elements C, O, Mn and Cu in the CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite. The presence of carbon is due to the addition of GQD before the CuMnO2 formation step. The inserted table is the analysis table of weight percentage and atomic percentage of elements. Also, elemental mapping analyses of C, Cu, Mn, and O are shown in Fig. 4b (inset). Comparing the results of EDX mapping of CuMnO2 and CuMnO2/GQD nanocrystals and considering that the dye density distribution depends on the distribution of different elements, it can be seen that in both synthesized materials all elements of Cu, Mn and O are homogeneously distributed.

The XRD patterns for CuMnO2 and CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposites were displayed in Fig. 4(c). The XRD patterns are well indexed to CuMnO2 (JCPDS 50–0860) [45]. GQD peak at approximately 2θ = 25° is not visible because it is less intense than other diffraction peaks. However, the X-ray diffraction pattern of CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposites is similar to that of CuMnO2, except that the intensity of the peaks is reduced due to the presence of GQD.

Investigation of the functional groups of nanocomposites was performed by FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 4(d) illustrates FT-IR spectra of CuMnO2 and CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposites. The absorption bands at 3350 and 1724 cm−1 can be associated to the OH and CO stretching vibrations, and the band of 1072 cm−1 is probably related to the COC stretching vibration, in the FT-IR spectrum of CuMnO2/GQD [47]. These peaks are not seen in the CuMnO2 spectrum, demonstrating the existence of GQD in the CuMnO2/GQD. Fig. 4(e) displays the Raman spectrum of the CuMnO2/GQD sample. The strong peak at 681.8 cm−1 and two weak peaks at 327.0 and 381.4 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the CuMnO2 structure. The peaks at 1351.0 and 1591.2 cm−1 related to the D and G bands of carbon in GQD confirm the presence of graphene quantum dot in the CuMnO2/GQD sample.

The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm of the CuMnO2/GQD was shown in Fig. 4(f). CuMnO2/GQD represents the type IV isotherm with the hysteresis ring, that is a feature of nanoparticles/mesoporous [49]. The specific surface area calculated from the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm was 145.5 m2 g−1 based on BET method. This is due to the combination of GQDs and CuMnO2 crystals in the structure of the electrode material and the finer material due to the role of graphene quantum dot as the nucleus for the formation of CuMnO2. Increasing the surface area has more active sites and improves charge transfer, which can effectively improve the capability to store energy. These results confirm the result of the BJH pore size distribution (inset of Fig. 4(f)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of electrochemical behavior of synthesized nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
To assess the electrochemical features of the nanocomposites as an active material to fabricate a supercapacitor, CV technique at different scan rates, EIS and GCD at constant currents were applied. Fig. 5 (a) demonstrations the CVs of the CuMnO2 and CuMnO2/GQD electrodes in the range of 0 to 0.5 V with a scan rate as 40 mV s−1. In order to make a logical comparison between the currents, the measured currents were normalized to the mass of active substance coated on the substrate. The following equation is used to compute the capacity through charge-discharge diagrams:
(3)
C
s p
=
I ×
Δ
t
m

In this regard, Csp is the specific capacity (C g−1), m is the mass of active substance coated on the substrate (g), I is the discharge current (A), and ∆t is the discharge time (s) [50]. At first glance, the significant increase in current of the CuMnO2/GQD and CuMnO2 electrodes compared to bare nickel foam is quite obvious. This indicates a very small effect of the substrate on the capacity.

Cyclic voltammograms of CuMnO2/GQD and CuMnO2 electrodes show clear redox peaks indicating their faradaic battery-type performance. The cathodic and anodic peaks appearing in the range of 0.2 to 0.4 V can be related to the Faradaic reactions of Cu and Mn with the participation of hydroxide ions due to the reversible redox reaction between MO/M-OH (M signifies copper and manganese) and OH− [45]. The CuMnO2/GQD electrode has a stronger oxidation–reduction peak than CuMnO2 due to the increased electrochemical activity owing to the synergic of properties of CuMnO2 and GQD, its high area and conductivity.

GCD curves of these three electrodes are displayed in Fig. 5(b). As shown the charge/discharge curves are completely out of the triangular shape that is specific to EDLC electrodes and clearly show the charge storage through Faradaic redox reactions. A displayed the most of the capacity is in the range of 0.1–0.4 V. This confirms the results of CV.

The specific capacity of the CuMnO2/GQD and the CuMnO2 electrodes were calculated to be 520.2 and 381.5 C g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, respectively. The CuMnO2/GQD electrode has a much better electrochemical behavior than the CuMnO2 electrode. The significant increase in capacity is due to the simultaneous use of CuMnO2 and GQD, which can provide the advantages of both materials. It can be concluded that the specific capacity of the CuMnO2 improved in the presence of GQD. Fig. 5(c) demonstrates the CV of the CuMnO2/GQD electrode at scan rate of 5 to 100 mV s−1 in 3.0 M potassium hydroxide. The slight change in the positions of the redox peaks with increasing scan rate is because of the diffusion polarization effect [51]. Increasing the scan rate in addition to increasing the current, caused a slight increase in the distance between the cathode and anode peaks that indicates rapid oxidation-reduction reactions occurring between the active substance/electrolyte and evidence of improved mass transfer, reversibility and rate capability [52–54]. CVs keep their form with increasing scan rate to 100 mVs−1. This phenomenon is evidence of improved mass transfer, reversibility in the CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite electrodes [55]. The CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite electrode has a better capacity than CuMnO2, besides it retains its electrochemical efficiency well even at very high current densities. Fig. 5(d), shows the capacity of the CuMnO2/GQD electrode at all current densities that are much higher than that of the CuMnO2 electrode under the same conditions.

The capacity of the CuMnO2/GQD and CuMnO2 electrodes have decreased by only 78.4% and 70.8% of their initial values, respectively, after changing the current density from 1 to 10 A g−1. The advantage is ascribed to the superior rate capability of the CuMnO2/GQD electrode, the shorter ion penetration path length, the improved electrical conductivity, and the larger specific surface area of the CuMnO2/GQD.

At small current densities, low concentration polarization lets the system complete the charging process in a longer time. In this case, ions near to the electrode are present in sufficient numbers and the oxidation-reduction reaction is facilitated and a high capacity is obtained. As presented in the Fig. 5(d), the capacity of the CuMnO2/GQD electrode shows less descent with increasing current density. This is caused by the fact that the CuMnO2 electrode exhibits good supercapacitor behavior at low current densities, while GQD performs better at high current densities. This difference is due to the different charge storage mechanism. Therefore, the combination of the two components creates a synergistic effect and improves supercapacitor performance. The higher capacity of the CuMnO2/GQD electrode at high current densities is owing to the low resistance of this composite, which facilitates ion and charge transfer in the electrode material.

Fig. 6 (a) and (b) show the GCD curves of the CuMnO2/GQD and CuMnO2 electrodes at different current densities of 1, 3, 5, 7, 10 A g−1. As these figures show the charge is stored through Faradaic redox reactions in the range of 0.0–0.42 V. This is completely consistent with data from CV technique.

For the CuMnO2/GQD electrode, a slight change in the shape of the CVs with increasing scan rate, as well as keeping the symmetrical shape of GCD curves at different current densities, indicates the very good reversibility and rate capability of this electrode. As can be seen, more scan rate increases, more the specific capacity decreases, which indicates the limited entrance of ions to the inner region of the electrode on a short time scale [56]. Naturally, as the current density increases, the discharge time decreases and ohmic drop in the system increases.

The reason of Ohmic drop is internal resistance (RS) of the electrode, which consists of the resistance of the electrical connections, the active substance, the ionic resistance of the electrolyte solution, and the ionic migration resistance of the electrode material. However, as can be seen, the ohmic drop of nanoporous electrode is small even at very high current densities such as 10 A g−1 (36 mA cm2), indicating high electrical conductivity and low RS of the electrode. High conductivity will reduce the capacity decline and will increase the efficiency along with the rate capability of the supercapacitors.

The cyclic life of electrode is one of the most significant parameters and criteria for evaluating the properties of supercapacitors, whether this material is suitable for practical applications of supercapacitors or not. For this purpose, the cyclic life of the CuMnO2 and CuMnO2/GQD electrodes was investigated during 5000 consecutive charge/discharge cycles at a current density of 10 A g−1.

As displayed in Fig. 6(c), the capacity of the CuMnO2/GQD electrode initially increases slightly, which can be related to the activation during cycling, which is commonly seen in initial cycles [57]. Subsequently, the capacity decreased slowly and after 5000 cycles reached 339.8 C g−1, equivalent to 83.3% of the initial capacity (the first charge/discharge cycle at 10 A g−1). This value indicates the very good cycle life of the CuMnO2/GQD electrode and is much better than the life cycle of the CuMnO2 electrode. Because in this electrode after 5000 cycles only 65.4% of the initial capacity has been recovered. The inserted figures correspond to the charge/discharge curves in the first and last cycles of each of electrodes.

This indicates that the CuMnO2/GQD electrode performed far better than the CuMnO2 electrode in capacity recovery. As a result, the CuMnO2/GQD electrode not only has a high capacity, but also a very good cycle life. Excellent life stability is ascribed to the stable nanostructures of CuMnO2/GQD and the strong mechanical bond between the CuMnO2/GQD and nickel foam nanostructures. Table 1 shows a comparison between the electrochemical behavior of the nanocomposite electrode synthesized in this work with other similar electrodes reported. High mass and surface capacity, outstanding rate capability and extraordinary cyclic stability of this electrode is related to its exceptional structural features.

To have more practical investigation and understand the electrical conductivity of the synthesized samples, EIS measurements were done in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz - 100 kHz. The Nyquist curves of the electrodes were displayed in Fig. 6(d). For both electrodes, a semicircle appears at high frequencies with a straight line at low frequencies. Semicircles are characterized by charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the interface between electrode/electrolyte and RS [77–79]. A semicircle with a larger diameter indicates a higher Rct of the material. The smaller semicircle belongs to the CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite material. This indicates less Rct in this nanocomposite. The Rct value was 0.73 Ω for CuMnO2/GQD and 0.94 Ω for CuMnO2. Based on the resulting Nyquist curves, the RS of the CuMnO2/GQD and CuMnO2 nanomaterials were calculated using the curve intersection with the real impedance axis. RS was 0.79 and 0.90 Ω for CuMnO2/GQD and CuMnO2 electrodes, respectively. The difference in RS of the electrodes is ascribed to the different conductivity of the electrodes. The lower conductivity of the CuMnO2 leads to a significant Rs, which justifies the less favorable behavior of this material. The line appearing at middle frequencies is characteristic of Warburg impedance and related to the resistance of electrolyte penetration in electrode structures [80,81]. The line with a sharp slope at low frequencies is characteristic of the electrode capacity, which appears vertically in ideal capacitors [82]. As can be seen, the linear part of the CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite curve is more vertical, has a smaller virtual impedance value than CuMnO2, and is actually closer to the ideal state. The presence of GQD as a conductive material in CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposites has led to enhanced electrochemical behavior. These observations can be ascribed to the fact that CuMnO2 has a high charge density inside the electrolyte, that leads to high resistance to ion transfer and hence its low capacity. However, CuMnO2/GQD nanocomposite has low resistance in the capacitive part of the Nyquist diagram because of the charge balance due to hybridization. All of these indicate better electrochemical behavior of CuMnO2/GQD than CuMnO2. These results indicate the very good performance of the CuMnO2/GQD electrode as a suitable electrode material for usage in supercapacitors.

The CuMnO2/GQD//AC asymmetric supercapacitor was assembled by using the battery-type CuMnO2/GQD electrode and EDLC AC electrode as the positive and negative electrodes, respectively. Supercapacitor energy density can be effectively increased by expansion of potential window by AC electrode and also by CuMnO2/GQD electrode and increasing specific capacitance due to the faradic redox reaction [49,83]. Using asymmetric configuration, the potential window in aqueous electrolytes can be raised above 1.5 V.

The CVs of the AC and CuMnO2/GQD electrodes after charge balancing at 40 mV s−1 scan rate in KOH 3.0 M were shown in Fig. 7 (a). The potential window of the AC electrode is from −1.0 to 0.0 V and the potential window of the CuMnO2/GQD electrode is from 0.0 to 0.5 V. The CV of the AC electrode is almost rectangular with no redox peaks, which is characteristic of EDLC behavior and is limited by the hydrogen gas release reaction. The shape of the CV of CuMnO2/GQD electrode indicates its battery-type behavior, which is limited by the oxygen release reaction.

The total voltage of the cell in the asymmetric configuration, if the charge balance between the positive and negative electrodes is observed, is equal to the total potential window of the electrodes. Therefore, the potential window of a CuMnO2/GQD//AC supercapacitor in KOH 3.0 M can be increased up to 1.5 V, which is greater than the potential window of commercially symmetric carbon-based supercapacitors in aqueous electrolytes. To investigate this issue, the CV of this supercapacitor was recorded in different potential windows.

Fig. 7(b) shows CVs of the supercapacitor at diverse scan rates from 5 to 100 mV s−1 in the constant voltage range of 0.0 to 1.5 V, which shows a slight change in the shape of the CVs with increasing the scan rate that indicates the reversibility and good rate capability of this supercapacitor. To further prove, the capacitance of the supercapacitor at different current densities was plotted. Also, to accurately determine the capacitance and energy density and power density of the supercapacitor, it is necessary to perform charge and discharge tests at different current densities. Fig. 7(c) shows GCD curves of the CuMnO2/GQD//AC supercapacitor at current densities of 1–10 A g−1 (5.7 to 57 mA cm−2). Keeping the symmetrical shape of the charge and discharge curves in the different current densities is another proof of the brilliant rate capability of this asymmetric supercapacitor. Also, a very small ohmic drop of the supercapacitor, even at very high current densities such as 10 A g−1 (57 mA cm−2), indicates high conductivity and low RS of this supercapacitor. High conductivity and low RS will reduce capacitance loss and lead to increased efficiency as well as improved supercapacitor rate capability. From the GCD curves and the following equations specific capacitance (CS) can be calculated [84]:
(4)
C S
=
2 I
∫
V
d t
m
V 2
Where V is the potential window, I is the constant current, Cs is the specific capacitance, and m is total mass placed on the positive and negative electrodes.

CuMnO2/GQD//AC supercapacitor shows mass capacities of 153.2, 136.0, 113.8, 94.7, and 76.8 F g−1 at current densities of 1, 3, 5, 7 and 10 A g−1, respectively. To prove the rate capability of the supercapacitor, the capacitance of the supercapacitor should be plotted in the different current densities. Fig. 7(d) is the diagram of the rate capability and shows the mass capacitance of the supercapacitor at diverse current densities. CuMnO2/GQD//AC supercapacitor shows the maximum capacitance of 153.2 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. Recovery of 50.1% of the initial capacitance, after 10 times the current density, indicates the excellent rate capability of this supercapacitor. Another advantage of using asymmetric configuration is that the integration of the Faradic process and the EDLC capacitive process can increase the cycle life of the entire system. This issue was investigated using 5000 consecutive charge/discharge cycles with a current density of 7 A g−1 and a voltage range of 0–1.5 V (inset to the Fig. 8 (a)). The capacitance of asymmetric CuMnO2/GQD//AC supercapacitor after 5000 charge/discharge was more than 86.7%, which shows its very good cycle life.

The Nyquist curves of the CuMnO2/GQD//AC supercapacitor before and after cycling stability were shown in Fig. 8(b). Rct values for CuMnO2/GQD before and after stability were 0.76 and 1.23 Ω, respectively. RS values were obtained before and after cycle life of 0.64 and 0.69 Ω, respectively. Specific energy and specific power are two very noteworthy factors in supercapacitors that determine their practical efficiency [16,85], which were obtained using the next equations:
(5)
E =
C ×
Δ
V 2
7.2
(6)
P =
3600 × E
Δ
t
Here E is the specific energy (Wh Kg−1), P is the specific power (W kg−1), C is the capacitance (F g−1), ∆V is the voltage range (V), and t is the discharge time (s). To prove the total performance of the CuMnO2/GQD//AC supercapacitor, its Ragone curve, which represents the specific energy and power of the supercapacitor at different current densities, was plotted in Fig. 8(c). The maximum specific energy for the CuMnO2/GQD//AC supercapacitor was obtained at a specific power of 1108.1 W kg−1 equal to 47.9 Wh Kg−1. Even the specific energy of 24.0 Wh Kg−1 at a specific power of 10,831.6 W kg−1 has a high specific capacitance and extensive electrochemical voltage window. The performance of this supercapacitor is much higher than new supercapacitors, such as: CuCo2O4@CuCo2O4//AC (18 Wh Kg−1) [86] CuO//AC(19.7 Wh Kg−1) [87], TP-MnS//AC (13.1 Wh Kg−1) [88], CuCo2O4@Co(OH)2//AG (19.2 Wh Kg−1) [89], GHCS-MnO2//GHCS (15 Wh Kg−1) [90], Co3O4@MnO2//MEGO (17.7 Wh Kg−1) [91], CNF@CuO NR (8.84 Wh Kg−1) [92], rGO/NF//CuMnO2-rGO/NF (37.5 Wh Kg−1) [65], CuCo2O4/CuO//RGO/Fe2O3 (33 Wh Kg−1) [93], MnO2/CNT//Fe2O3/CNT (45.8 Wh Kg−1) [94], NiMn@Mn-1 h//AC (41.6 Wh Kg−1) [95].

To show the true application of supercapacitors, two CuMnO2/GQD//AC supercapacitors were linked in series and used to turn on a green LED (2.7 V). As presented in Fig. 9 (a) and (b), the two asymmetric supercapacitors could easily keep an LED lamp on for more than 20 min.

In this work, a nanocomposite of GQD and CuMnO2 was synthesized. Then it was applied as an electrode material in supercapacitors. It was proved that these unique structures have a much higher mass capacitance than CuMnO2. This nanocomposite showed a great capacitance of 520.2 C g−1 in 3.0 M KOH electrolyte at a current density of 1 A g−1 and a good rate capability with a capacitance retention of 78.4% at a current density of 10 A g−1 vs. 1 A g−1 and made it possible to build asymmetric supercapacitors with high capacitance 153.2 F g−1 at current density of 1 A g−1 and specific energy of 47.9 Wh Kg−1 at specific power of 1108.1 W kg−1, in addition to excellent rate capability and cyclic stability with 86.7% initial capacitance recovery after 5000 consecutive cycles at current density 7 A g−1. Furthermore, turning on the LED for more than 20 min showed that the supercapacitor also has extraordinary performance.
